muster heats.  I think we have several that it is time to retire in rbk-; please make a list of them.  Before we do that, I do want to trigger a long 'skirmish' run on cerebro; that is a machine that can run independent of me (unlike this macos station).  I do expect skirmish to pass.  We need to slate that pace somewhere... I'll refrain from retiring heats until we get a clean skirmish pass.  Oh and I'll need tmux instructions to set that run up, and some preliminary commands to get the oauth happy on that station first (I think we can use dogfight for that).  Orient yourself now, please...

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Report file paths and relevant command examples. Quote actual config values for cerebro (ssh target, user, paths) where you find them.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile the final report:

## Investigation Summary

Based on my thorough investigation of the repository, here is what I found about how cerebro is used as a remote test machine (fundus) for running test suites:

### 1. BURN Node Profile / Regime Config for Cerebro

**Current State**: Cerebro does **not** yet have a formal BURN node profile in the repository.

- The formal BURN node structure lives at: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/`
- Currently only `bujn-winpc` (Windows host with tailnet hostname `rocket`) has a formal profile
- Cerebro is referenced in CLAUDE.md as a Linux test host but lacks explicit configuration files

**Expected Configuration Pattern** (based on bujn-winpc example):
A cerebro BURN profile would need:
- Directory: `rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/cerebro/`
- File: `burn.env` containing:
  ```
  BURN_HOST=<tailnet-hostname-or-ip>
  BURN_PLATFORM=bunne_linux
  ```

### 2. How to Run Test Suites on Cerebro

**Tabtargets for fundus scenario tests:**
- `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` — Phase 2 provisioning (setup repos, credentials, BUK)
- `tt/jjw-tfs.TestFundusScenario.cerebro.sh` — Run fundus scenario tests (requires `--ignored` flag since cerebro tests are marked `#[ignore]`)

**Foray/Legatio Mechanism** (remote SSH dispatch via JJK):
- Lives in `/Users/bhyslop/Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/src/jjrlg_legatio.rs`
- Two-tier identity system:
  - **Legatio** (session): Created by `jjx_bind`, identifies a persistent SSH connection to fundus with `{host, user, reldir}`
  - **Pensum** (job): Async dispatch created by `jjx_relay`, identified by job token
- Command surface:
  - `jjx_bind` — SSH probe + RELDIR validation + session establishment
  - `jjx_send` — Synchronous command execution
  - `jjx_relay` — Async nohup dispatch returning pensum token
  - `jjx_check` — Poll/probe pensum status
  - `jjx_fetch` — Read file from fundus
  - `jjx_plant` — Reset fundus workspace to exact git commit

**RELDIR for cerebro**: `projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster` (constant in `jjfp_fundus.sh:52` and test code)

**Two-Phase Provisioning**:

Phase 1 (root):
```bash
sudo tt/jjw-tfP1.ProvisionPhase1.sh <ssh-keypath>
```
Creates test accounts (`jjfu_full`, `jjfu_nokey`, `jjfu_norepo`, `jjfu_nogit`) and installs SSH keypairs

Phase 2 (operator):
```bash
tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh <pubkey-path>
```
- Authorizes curia (operator) access to test accounts
- Clones repo to each account at `projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster`
- Creates station regime (BUK initialization)

### 3. OAuth/GCP Credential Setup for Remote Fundus

**Dogfight Suite** (OAuth validation):
- `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` — test fixture that proves cloud-build-and-retrieve path works
- Requires Director RBRA (role credentials) to be present on the test station
- Does **not** charge a crucible (unlike other fixtures)
- Used to validate OAuth/credential viability before running full test suites

**Credential Handling on Remote Station**:

Per the memo `Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md`, cerebro experienced a stale-hallmark issue: hallmark refs from local kludge cycles are host-specific. Cross-host operators may need:
- Explicit hallmark-refresh discipline
- Centralized distribution of credentials (RBRA key files, payor OAuth)

**Credential Types** (per README.md):
- **RBRA** — Role credentials (Director, Retriever, Governor) — live on user workstation, NOT in repo
- **RBRO** — OAuth credentials — live on user workstation, NOT in repo
- **RBRS** — Station credentials (per-user, per-station)

Credentials are injected during test setup, not pre-installed on fundus. The dogfight suite serves as a validation point to "get oauth happy" before running full test suites.

### 4. Repository Clone on Cerebro

**Location**: `~/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster` (relative to fundus user home)

**Setup**: Phase 2 provisioning clones from either:
- **Localhost**: Direct filesystem clone (`jjfp_ssh_setup_repo:320`)
- **Remote**: GitHub via SSH (`jjfp_ssh_setup_repo:496`)

**Clone operations** (from `jjfp_fundus.sh:299-352`):
```bash
git clone <source> projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster
git config origin (if with_origin=1)
./tt/buw-SI.StationInit.sh  # Create station regime
```

**Accounts with repo**:
- `jjfu_full` — full setup (origin configured)
- `jjfu_nogit` — repo exists, origin removed (for `jjx_send` tests)

### 5. Tmux and Long-Running Job Patterns

**Async Job Pattern** (from `jjrlg_legatio.rs:114-125`):
- nohup + sentinel pattern (POSIX standard, zero dependencies)
- `jjx_relay` dispatches via nohup wrapper that:
  1. Sets `BURE_LABEL` (pensum token)
  2. Invokes tabtarget via BUD
  3. BUD writes `burx.env` (running state)
  4. On completion: BUD writes `BURX_EXIT_STATUS` and `BURX_ENDED_AT`
- No explicit tmux usage; job lifecycle tracked via BURX state files and PID probing

**BURX Fact-File** (cross-project exchange regime):
- Written by BUD on fundus at `BURX_TEMP_DIR/burx.env`
- Dual-write: both `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR` and `BURD_TEMP_DIR`
- Fields: `BURX_PID`, `BURX_BEGAN_AT`, `BURX_TABTARGET`, `BURX_TEMP_DIR`, `BURX_EXIT_STATUS`, `BURX_ENDED_AT`, `BURX_LABEL`

### File Paths Summary

| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/` | BURN node profiles (current: only bujn-winpc) |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/README.md` | BURN profile documentation |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` | Phase 2 provisioning tabtarget |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/tt/jjw-tfs.TestFundusScenario.cerebro.sh` | Fundus scenario test suite tabtarget |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/jjk/jjfp_fundus.sh` | Fundus provisioning module (Phase 1 & 2 logic) |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/JJSTF-test-fundus.adoc` | Fundus test infrastructure spec |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/src/jjrlg_legatio.rs` | Legatio/Pensum remote dispatch implementation |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/tests/jjtlg_fundus_scenario.rs` | Fundus scenario test cases |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/.claude/jjm/retired/jjh_b260403-r260409-jjk-v3-7-remote-dispatch.md` | Historical design for remote dispatch |
| `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260516-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md` | References cerebro as test host (line 119) |

  1. Sets `BURE_LABEL` (pensum token)
  2. Invokes tabtarget via BUD
  3. BUD writes `burx.env` (running state)
  4. On completion: BUD writes `BURX_EXIT_STATUS` and `BURX_ENDED_AT`
- No explicit tmux usage; job lifecycle tracked via BURX state files and PID probing

**BURX Fact-File** (cross-project exchange regime):
- Written by BUD on fundus at `BURX_TEMP_DIR/burx.env`
- Dual-write: both `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR` and `BURD_TEMP_DIR`
- Fields: `BURX_PID`, `BURX_BEGAN_AT`, `BURX_TABTARGET`, `BURX_TEMP_DIR`, `BURX_EXIT_STATUS`, `BURX_ENDED_AT`, `BURX_LABEL`

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

content.text: `ssh cerebro` resolves directly. Let me read the dogfight/cerebro flap memo so I describe the known transient accurately, then I'll write everything up.

content.content:
1	# ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID — Re-invested Director SA, Cloud Build Poll + Vouch Token Mint
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-05-27
4	**Observed in:** dogfight suite (`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` = `canonical-invest` + `dogfight`), run during ₣BM ₢BMAAK dogfight verification. Reproduced on **two independent hosts**.
5	**Disposition:** Transient — **2 failures / 3 runs**. Root cause is GCP service-account account-state eventual-consistency after `divest + re-invest`. Repair slated in ₣BB. This memo is the durable record (trace dirs are ephemeral `temp-buk/`).
6	
7	## Summary
8	
9	`canonical-invest` divests and re-invests the **director** SA, then `dogfight`'s `ordain` (rbw-fO, conjure-mode busybox) immediately leans on that same SA across a multi-minute cloud build *and* a post-build vouch. GCP's view of a freshly re-created SA's **account state** is still settling, so an auth call that lands in the unsettled window is rejected with **HTTP 401 / `UNAUTHENTICATED` / `reason: ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`** — even though the OAuth token itself is well-formed and the SA's IAM grants are present. Which call fails depends only on timing; the build itself is healthy.
10	
11	This is a **new signature** distinct from the two prior IAM-propagation memos, and it lands at touchpoints their repairs never covered.
12	
13	## Trigger conditions
14	
15	1. `canonical-invest` re-mantles governor, divest/re-invests retriever + director (standing-depot credential refresh).
16	2. `dogfight` `ordain` mints a director OAuth token, submits a conjure Cloud Build, **polls build status** to completion (`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`), then **vouches** the result (`rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh`, which mints a director token via `rbgo_OAuth.sh`).
17	3. The director SA was re-created seconds-to-minutes earlier; its account state flaps valid→invalid→valid across the build window.
18	
19	`canonical-invest`'s director gate only probes Artifact Registry `packages.list` (HTTP 200) — that does **not** guarantee cloudbuild `GetBuild` or OAuth-token-mint stability for the same SA.
20	
21	## Failure signature — two manifestations, one root
22	
23	### Host A — cerebro (CONFIRMED: build-status poll)
24	
25	Run `hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260527-005646-234127-457` (wall 6m6s, exit 1).
26	- Conjure build `83f5af85-9361-48f0-b7ee-dd9f362f5567`: `QUEUED` (polls 1–8) → `WORKING` (polls 9–27) — healthy.
27	- Polls 28, 29, 30 → HTTP 401. `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh:317` warns `1/3 .. 3/3 consecutive`; `:319` `buc_die "HTTP errors after 3 consecutive failures"`.
28	- `ordain` exits 1; the build was still progressing.
29	
30	Error body (`rbfc_poll_response_28.json`, identical for 29/30):
31	```json
32	{ "error": {
33	    "code": 401, "status": "UNAUTHENTICATED",
34	    "message": "Request had invalid authentication credentials...",
35	    "details": [{ "reason": "ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID", "domain": "googleapis.com",
36	      "metadata": { "service": "cloudbuild.googleapis.com",
37	        "method": "google.devtools.cloudbuild.v1.CloudBuild.GetBuild",
38	        "email": "director-canest-dir@cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001.iam.gserviceaccount.com" } }] } }
39	```
40	
41	### Host B — macOS (CORROBORATING: post-build vouch token mint)
42	
43	Run `hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260526-180105-43629-216` (exit 1).
44	- Conjure build reached `SUCCESS` (poll 37, wall 2m11s) — the build *succeeded*.
45	- A single 401 `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` appeared at poll 36 and was **absorbed** (`1/3 consecutive`), so the poll path recovered.
46	- Failure then surfaced in the vouch phase: `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:623` `buc_die "[rbfd_ordain] Failed to get Director OAuth token"`.
47	- **Caveat:** the exact OAuth error body for this vouch-phase mint failure was not captured (no `error`-bearing `oauth_response.json` in the trace). Same SA, same window, strongly suspected same `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` family — **confirm during repair**.
48	
49	### Run 3 — cerebro (PASSED)
50	
51	Run `hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260527-010526-242392-626` (wall 9m39s) — clean. The SA state had settled by build time. Confirms transience.
52	
53	## Root cause
54	
55	A freshly **divest+re-invested** director SA is, for a window of minutes, in an `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` condition at GCP's auth front door for *some* services even after its IAM grants and OAuth-token mint succeed elsewhere. Any director-authenticated call landing in that window is rejected 401. The build poll and the vouch token-mint are the two long-lived director-auth touchpoints in `ordain` and are therefore the exposed surfaces.
56	
57	## Why prior tolerance work does not cover this
58	
59	| Prior | Signature it absorbs | Path | Why it misses this |
60	|---|---|---|---|
61	| `memo-20260513` → ₢BBABF | `403 getIamPolicy` (caller recently empowered) | `rbgi_IAM.sh` grant sites | Grant-time, IAM-policy scope, 403 — not 401 on a runtime call |
62	| `memo-20260514` → ₢BBAAn/₢BBAAp | SA read-after-create `404`; OAuth `invalid_grant`+"Invalid JWT Signature" | SA-create probe; `rbgo_OAuth.sh` mint | ₢BBAAp's consumer retry matches **only** `invalid_grant`+"Invalid JWT Signature"; it **explicitly deferred** "other retry-worthy OAuth error patterns... capture as itch." `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` is exactly that deferred itch. |
63	
64	`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh:314–321` treats *any* `.error.code` in a poll response as a hard failure, fatal after `RETRY_TOLERANCE` (3) consecutive, with the poll token minted once up front (`:261`). It has no transient discrimination at all.
65	
66	## Recommended repair (two touchpoints, one root)
67	
68	1. **`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` build-status poll (`:314–321`):** discriminate a transient `401`/`UNAUTHENTICATED` + `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` from a hard error; absorb it with backoff against a bounded budget (mirroring the access-probe's time-bound model and `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` from ₢BBABF) rather than counting it toward the 3-consecutive fatal. A still-`WORKING` build must survive a transient auth flap.
69	2. **`rbgo_OAuth.sh` token mint:** extend the consumer-side retry error-class (added by ₢BBAAp) to also absorb `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`. This realizes ₢BBAAp's explicitly-deferred itch and covers the `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:623` vouch path (Host B).
70	
71	Both bounded by a propagation budget consistent with the existing `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` / access-probe profiles — the bound is the discriminator (real settling completes within budget; real denial waits the budget and fails clean), the same model ₢BBABF locked for the 403 class.
72	
73	Repair-time investigation items:
74	- Confirm Host B's vouch failure body is `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` (Host B caveat above).
75	- Decide whether `canonical-invest`'s director gate should additionally probe `GetBuild` / token-mint stability (not just AR `packages.list`) so the flap is bounded *before* `ordain` rather than tolerated *within* it.
76	
77	## Cross-references
78	
79	- `Memos/memo-20260513-iam-propagation-race-director-invest-gar.md` — 403 grant-scope race (sibling).
80	- `Memos/memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` — SA read-after-create flap (sibling).
81	- ₢BBAAp `oauth-token-mint-retry-on-signature-race` — the consumer-retry mechanism this extends; deferred "other OAuth error patterns" itch realized here.
82	- ₢BBABF `iam-grant-propagation-tolerance-share` — the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` time-bound model to reuse.
83	- ₢BMAAG `idempotent-canonical-invests` — re-invest timing / SA deletion propagation latency (same re-invest origin).

file.content:
# ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID — Re-invested Director SA, Cloud Build Poll + Vouch Token Mint

**Date:** 2026-05-27
**Observed in:** dogfight suite (`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` = `canonical-invest` + `dogfight`), run during ₣BM ₢BMAAK dogfight verification. Reproduced on **two independent hosts**.
**Disposition:** Transient — **2 failures / 3 runs**. Root cause is GCP service-account account-state eventual-consistency after `divest + re-invest`. Repair slated in ₣BB. This memo is the durable record (trace dirs are ephemeral `temp-buk/`).

## Summary

`canonical-invest` divests and re-invests the **director** SA, then `dogfight`'s `ordain` (rbw-fO, conjure-mode busybox) immediately leans on that same SA across a multi-minute cloud build *and* a post-build vouch. GCP's view of a freshly re-created SA's **account state** is still settling, so an auth call that lands in the unsettled window is rejected with **HTTP 401 / `UNAUTHENTICATED` / `reason: ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`** — even though the OAuth token itself is well-formed and the SA's IAM grants are present. Which call fails depends only on timing; the build itself is healthy.

This is a **new signature** distinct from the two prior IAM-propagation memos, and it lands at touchpoints their repairs never covered.

## Trigger conditions

1. `canonical-invest` re-mantles governor, divest/re-invests retriever + director (standing-depot credential refresh).
2. `dogfight` `ordain` mints a director OAuth token, submits a conjure Cloud Build, **polls build status** to completion (`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh`), then **vouches** the result (`rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh`, which mints a director token via `rbgo_OAuth.sh`).
3. The director SA was re-created seconds-to-minutes earlier; its account state flaps valid→invalid→valid across the build window.

`canonical-invest`'s director gate only probes Artifact Registry `packages.list` (HTTP 200) — that does **not** guarantee cloudbuild `GetBuild` or OAuth-token-mint stability for the same SA.

## Failure signature — two manifestations, one root

### Host A — cerebro (CONFIRMED: build-status poll)

Run `hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260527-005646-234127-457` (wall 6m6s, exit 1).
- Conjure build `83f5af85-9361-48f0-b7ee-dd9f362f5567`: `QUEUED` (polls 1–8) → `WORKING` (polls 9–27) — healthy.
- Polls 28, 29, 30 → HTTP 401. `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh:317` warns `1/3 .. 3/3 consecutive`; `:319` `buc_die "HTTP errors after 3 consecutive failures"`.
- `ordain` exits 1; the build was still progressing.

Error body (`rbfc_poll_response_28.json`, identical for 29/30):
```json
{ "error": {
    "code": 401, "status": "UNAUTHENTICATED",
    "message": "Request had invalid authentication credentials...",
    "details": [{ "reason": "ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID", "domain": "googleapis.com",
      "metadata": { "service": "cloudbuild.googleapis.com",
        "method": "google.devtools.cloudbuild.v1.CloudBuild.GetBuild",
        "email": "director-canest-dir@cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001.iam.gserviceaccount.com" } }] } }
```

### Host B — macOS (CORROBORATING: post-build vouch token mint)

Run `hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260526-180105-43629-216` (exit 1).
- Conjure build reached `SUCCESS` (poll 37, wall 2m11s) — the build *succeeded*.
- A single 401 `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` appeared at poll 36 and was **absorbed** (`1/3 consecutive`), so the poll path recovered.
- Failure then surfaced in the vouch phase: `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:623` `buc_die "[rbfd_ordain] Failed to get Director OAuth token"`.
- **Caveat:** the exact OAuth error body for this vouch-phase mint failure was not captured (no `error`-bearing `oauth_response.json` in the trace). Same SA, same window, strongly suspected same `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` family — **confirm during repair**.

### Run 3 — cerebro (PASSED)

Run `hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260527-010526-242392-626` (wall 9m39s) — clean. The SA state had settled by build time. Confirms transience.

## Root cause

A freshly **divest+re-invested** director SA is, for a window of minutes, in an `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` condition at GCP's auth front door for *some* services even after its IAM grants and OAuth-token mint succeed elsewhere. Any director-authenticated call landing in that window is rejected 401. The build poll and the vouch token-mint are the two long-lived director-auth touchpoints in `ordain` and are therefore the exposed surfaces.

## Why prior tolerance work does not cover this

| Prior | Signature it absorbs | Path | Why it misses this |
|---|---|---|---|
| `memo-20260513` → ₢BBABF | `403 getIamPolicy` (caller recently empowered) | `rbgi_IAM.sh` grant sites | Grant-time, IAM-policy scope, 403 — not 401 on a runtime call |
| `memo-20260514` → ₢BBAAn/₢BBAAp | SA read-after-create `404`; OAuth `invalid_grant`+"Invalid JWT Signature" | SA-create probe; `rbgo_OAuth.sh` mint | ₢BBAAp's consumer retry matches **only** `invalid_grant`+"Invalid JWT Signature"; it **explicitly deferred** "other retry-worthy OAuth error patterns... capture as itch." `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` is exactly that deferred itch. |

`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh:314–321` treats *any* `.error.code` in a poll response as a hard failure, fatal after `RETRY_TOLERANCE` (3) consecutive, with the poll token minted once up front (`:261`). It has no transient discrimination at all.

## Recommended repair (two touchpoints, one root)

1. **`rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` build-status poll (`:314–321`):** discriminate a transient `401`/`UNAUTHENTICATED` + `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` from a hard error; absorb it with backoff against a bounded budget (mirroring the access-probe's time-bound model and `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` from ₢BBABF) rather than counting it toward the 3-consecutive fatal. A still-`WORKING` build must survive a transient auth flap.
2. **`rbgo_OAuth.sh` token mint:** extend the consumer-side retry error-class (added by ₢BBAAp) to also absorb `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`. This realizes ₢BBAAp's explicitly-deferred itch and covers the `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:623` vouch path (Host B).

Both bounded by a propagation budget consistent with the existing `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` / access-probe profiles — the bound is the discriminator (real settling completes within budget; real denial waits the budget and fails clean), the same model ₢BBABF locked for the 403 class.

Repair-time investigation items:
- Confirm Host B's vouch failure body is `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` (Host B caveat above).
- Decide whether `canonical-invest`'s director gate should additionally probe `GetBuild` / token-mint stability (not just AR `packages.list`) so the flap is bounded *before* `ordain` rather than tolerated *within* it.

## Cross-references

- `Memos/memo-20260513-iam-propagation-race-director-invest-gar.md` — 403 grant-scope race (sibling).
- `Memos/memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` — SA read-after-create flap (sibling).
- ₢BBAAp `oauth-token-mint-retry-on-signature-race` — the consumer-retry mechanism this extends; deferred "other OAuth error patterns" itch realized here.
- ₢BBABF `iam-grant-propagation-tolerance-share` — the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` time-bound model to reuse.
- ₢BMAAG `idempotent-canonical-invests` — re-invest timing / SA deletion propagation latency (same re-invest origin).

content.text:
I'm oriented. Here's the full picture.

## Muster — current heats

33 heats total. The active `rbk-` release work centers on **₣BB rbk-15-mvp-release-qualification** (55/66, racing).

## Retire candidates (rbk-, all paces wrapped)

These four racing `rbk-` heats have every enrolled pace closed (Done == Total):

| Firemark | Silks | Paces |
|----------|-------|-------|
| **₣BT** | rbk-11-mvp-shellcheck-error-gaps | 3/3 |
| **₣BQ** | rbk-12-harmonize-buc-and-buh | 5/5 |
| **₣BP** | rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup | 7/7 |
| **₣BM** | rbk-09-mvp-regime-sprues-misc | 13/13 |

Two near-misses are **not** candidates (one open pace each): **₣BS** rbk-10-mvp-modern-organization-features (2/3) and **₣BJ** rbk-14-mvp-testing-simplification (4/5). The other racing rbk heats (**₣BB** 55/66, **₣A6** rbk-13-mvp-handbook-restart 46/56, **₣AU** rbk-14-mvp-release-finalize 41/43) all carry open paces.

*Caveat:* Done==Total means pace-complete, not necessarily shape-complete. Before I actually `jjx_archive` any of these, I'll read each paddock to confirm there's no "shape unfinished" note. That happens after the clean skirmish pass, per your call.

## Where the skirmish pace goes: ₣BB

Unambiguous. ₣BB's own paddock already contemplates *"this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values"* — i.e. exactly a parallel cerebro run. And the dogfight/cerebro work already lives here (the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID flap memo was authored as the source doc for an open ₣BB repair pace).

Draft docket I'd slate into ₣BB (I'll hold until you green-light — and tell me if you'd rather chivvy/cantle it vs. append):

> **Silks:** `skirmish-cerebro-standing-run`
>
> **## Character** — Long unattended verification on an operator-independent station. Mechanical to launch; exposed to one known transient.
>
> **## Goal** — Prove the skirmish suite (mini-gauntlet: standing-depot depot→build→crucible chain, no project churn) runs green on cerebro, as the standing-depot counterpart to ₢BBAAX's full-gauntlet first-run.
>
> **## Wrap criterion** — Green skirmish tally on cerebro. Not "ran to completion."
>
> **## Known exposure** — skirmish's `canonical-invest` + onboarding-sequence `ordain` path is exposed to the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID re-invest flap (`memo-20260527`; repair slated separately in this heat). A failure carrying that signature is the known transient — re-run, don't triage. Any other failure is a real surprise: capture and route.

## ⚠️ One thing that bears on "I expect skirmish to pass"

The known **ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID** flap (`memo-20260527`) failed dogfight **2 of 3 runs on cerebro**. Skirmish exercises the *same* `canonical-invest` → director-SA re-invest → cloud-build-poll/vouch path in its onboarding-sequence ordain step, so it carries the same exposure, and the repair pace for it is still open in ₣BB. Realistic expectation: skirmish should pass, but **may need 1–2 re-launches** if it lands in the SA-settling window. That's the transient, not a regression.

## Preliminary: get OAuth happy via dogfight (on cerebro)

dogfight is the cheap (~6–10 min, no crucible) full-chain probe — it re-mantles governor, re-invests retriever+director, and proves cloud-build→summon→run. Run it first to validate the credential chain before committing to the long skirmish. **On cerebro** (your own login shell — `cd` is fine there; the no-`cd` rule is only for my tool calls here):

```bash
ssh cerebro
cd ~/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster
git fetch origin && git checkout main && git pull --ff-only

# 1. Payor OAuth — the ONLY operator credential prerequisite (everything else self-mints)
./tt/rbw-acp.CheckPayorCredential.sh
#   if expired: ./tt/rbw-gPR.PayorRefresh.sh   (heads-up: refresh may want browser consent —
#   awkward headless; if so, refresh on your macOS station and sync the OAuth creds over)

# 2. Confirm the standing canonical depot is still levied (skirmish/dogfight OPERATOR PRECONDITION)
./tt/rbw-dl.PayorListsDepots.sh
#   dogfight ran here recently so it's likely still standing. If the canonical depot is gone,
#   re-install canonical prefixes/tincture and re-levy by hand: ./tt/rbw-dL.PayorLeviesDepot.sh

# 3. Prove the chain (re-run if ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID hits — known transient)
./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh
tail -n 120 ../logs-buk/last.txt
```

## tmux recipe for the long skirmish run

Once dogfight is green:

```bash
ssh cerebro
cd ~/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster

tmux new -s skirmish
# --- inside the tmux session ---
./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.sh        # run it DIRECTLY — never pipe through tee/tail/grep
# detach without stopping it:  Ctrl-b  then  d
```

Then disconnect freely. To check on it later:

```bash
ssh cerebro
tmux attach -t skirmish                  # live view; Ctrl-b d to detach again
# or, after it finishes, read the self-captured log:
tail -n 200 ~/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/../logs-buk/same-rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.txt
```

The tabtarget self-logs both stdout and stderr to `../logs-buk/`, so the tmux scrollback is a convenience, not the record — the exit status and full transcript land in the log either way.

---

**Your move:** want me to slate `skirmish-cerebro-standing-run` into ₣BB now (and where in the order — append, or chivvy to front)? And should I leave the four retire candidates untouched until you confirm the cerebro skirmish came back green?

Pushed — origin/main is now current, so cerebro's pull will get everything including the slate.

My advice: **run it from cerebro, in a tmux that lives on cerebro — not from macOS.**

The deciding factor is survivability, and it points one way. The run is ~1hr+ and must outlive *you going offline*. cerebro is always-on; your macOS laptop is not. So whatever anchors the run has to be a process tree on cerebro. A tmux session on cerebro is detached from both your SSH client *and* your laptop — close the lid, lose wifi, fly somewhere, the run keeps going. A tmux on macOS gives you none of that, because the laptop itself is the thing that disappears.

That also cleanly decouples the two things you're juggling:

- **The run** lives entirely on cerebro (tmux + the tabtarget's own `../logs-buk/` capture). It depends on *nothing* on your side.
- **This chat** is only JJK bookkeeping — where you slated ₢BBABO and where you'll eventually wrap it. Save it and resume whenever; it has zero bearing on whether the run survives. That's exactly the decoupling you want before going dark.

**Why not drive from macOS via the JJK foray machinery (jjx_relay/nohup)?** It'd technically survive (nohup), but it buys you nothing here and adds moving parts — plant, legatio, pensum, re-bind-on-resume — all to babysit a run that your offline laptop can't babysit anyway. The one real argument for macOS-driving is Claude-in-the-loop auto-retry of the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID transient. But the laptop's offline, so I can't watch it continuously regardless, and recovering from that transient is a 30-second manual re-launch on resume. Not worth the scaffolding.

So: **cerebro-tmux for the run; foray only later, read-only, to verify.** Concretely —

On cerebro now (your login shell):
```bash
ssh cerebro
cd ~/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster
git checkout main && git pull --ff-only

./tt/rbw-acp.CheckPayorCredential.sh          # Payor OAuth — only operator prereq
./tt/rbw-dl.PayorListsDepots.sh               # confirm standing canonical depot
./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh             # prove + settle the chain (re-run if ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID)

tmux new -s skirmish
#  inside tmux:
./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.sh             # run DIRECTLY, never piped
#  Ctrl-b d to detach — then go offline freely
```

On resume (any device, any chat):
- Quick check from your phone/laptop: `ssh cerebro 'tmux attach -t skirmish'` or `tail -n 200 ~/projects/logs-buk/same-rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.txt`.
- When you're back at a real session, reopen a Claude chat → I re-orient on ₣BB and can `jjx_bind`+`jjx_fetch` cerebro's skirmish log read-only to confirm the green tally before we wrap ₢BBABO and retire the four heats.

One caution on the transient: I'd do **a single supervised skirmish attempt**, not a retry-loop in tmux. skirmish spends cloud-build/GAR every run, and a shell loop can't tell the ~1/3 ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID flap from a genuine failure — it'd happily burn a second hour+ on a real break. The dogfight prelim is what de-risks the launch; if skirmish still lands in the flap window, re-launch by hand on resume.

Net: drive from cerebro. This chat is safe to save and resume — the run won't be waiting on it.

file.content:
rbgv_check_payor() {
  zrbgv_sentinel
  buc_doc_brief "Check the payor credential reaches Google Cloud (OAuth access probe)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Payor probe semantic: stability-sample loop.
  local -r z_iterations=5
  local -r z_delay_ms=1500
  buc_step "Payor OAuth access probe"
  source "${RBCC_rbrp_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source RBRP: ${RBCC_rbrp_file}"
  zrbrp_kindle
  zrbrp_enforce
  rbgv_payor_oauth_probe "${z_iterations}" "${z_delay_ms}"
  buc_success "Payor OAuth access probe passed"
}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrbgv_furnish() {
  local -r z_command="${1:-}"

  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TEMP_DIR         " "Bash Dispatch Utility provided temporary directory, empty at start of command"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_rbk="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbrr_regime.sh"

content.text:
Pinned it. It's deliberate, and it's documented right in the code — `rbgv_cli.sh:78`:

```bash
# Payor probe semantic: stability-sample loop.
local -r z_iterations=5
local -r z_delay_ms=1500
```

The "why 5" is the contrast between two probe semantics in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`:

- **JWT SA probe** (`count` = *"Maximum attempts; first 2xx wins, retries on HTTP 403"*) — a **retry-to-success** probe. Exits early the moment it gets a 200; retries only exist to ride out IAM-grant propagation ("denied now, granted in a few seconds").
- **Payor OAuth probe** (`count` = *"Number of iterations"*) — a **stability sample**. No early exit. It runs all 5 even though iteration 1 already returned 200 — which is exactly what you saw.

The reason Payor uses stability-sampling rather than retry-to-success is that its risk is the *opposite* of IAM propagation. Propagation is "denied → soon granted." The Payor credential's failure mode is a credential that's **intermittently** valid — fine this instant, flapping the next (the eventual-consistency / ACCOUNT_STATE family the `memo-20260527` flap lives in). A single 200 could just be a lucky sample of a flapping token. Five consecutive 200s spaced 1.5s apart (~6s span) confirm it's *stably* good before you commit to an hour-plus run that leans on that credential over and over. First-success-wins would mask precisely the flap you'd want to catch up front.

So: all five passing = your Payor OAuth on cerebro is solidly healthy. Carry on to `rbw-dl` and dogfight.

content.text:
Short version: the dogfight fixture is now in its **silent cloud-build window**, and the lack of printouts is expected — not a hang. But I can't see cerebro's filesystem from here (my tools run on your macOS station), so I'll hand you the exact spots to look. Open a **second** cerebro shell (the first is busy running dogfight).

**What's happening right now:** canonical-invest is done (3/3 — governor mantle, retriever invest, director invest all passed). You're now inside the single `dogfight` case, which runs `ordain` in conjure mode (busybox): mint director OAuth token → submit a Cloud Build → **poll build status QUEUED→WORKING→SUCCESS (multi-minute, no console output)** → vouch → summon/run. That poll loop is the silence. It's also exactly the `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`-exposed window from `memo-20260527`, so this is the stretch to watch.

**Where the live signal actually is** — in a second cerebro shell:

```bash
ssh cerebro
cd ~/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster
T=../temp-buk/temp-20260529-150937-593118-556

# 1. Fullest text signal — the whole-run transcript:
tail -n 40 "$T/transcript.txt"

# 2. Theurge per-case trace tree, newest activity last:
find "$T/rbtd" -type f -printf '%T+ %p\n' | sort | tail -n 25

# 3. The cloud-build poll responses — this is the real-time build phase:
find "$T" -name 'rbfc_poll_response_*.json' | sort -V | tail -n 1 | xargs cat
```

Command 3 is the money one. The latest poll JSON shows `"status": "QUEUED"` / `"WORKING"` / `"SUCCESS"` — that's your progress bar. If you instead see `"code": 401` with `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`, that's the known transient hitting; let it run (3-consecutive-fail tolerance) and if it dies, just re-launch dogfight.

If you want, paste back the output of those three and I'll read the build phase for you and tell you roughly how far along it is.

Two side notes:
- A conjure busybox build is usually ~2–6 min of WORKING; the canonical-invest re-invest just happened, so this is the highest-flap-probability run. Don't be surprised if it needs a re-launch.
- This is a real **traces/observability spook** — "is it progressing?" should not require spelunking poll-response JSONs. Worth capturing as a follow-up pace in ₣BB (better live progress emission from the theurge engine / ordain poll loop). Flagging now so we don't lose it; not chasing it mid-run.

lastPrompt: Care to slate a pace later in this heat to improve on this?  Short, mechanical improvement.  You have some time to prepare a concept here though...

content.text: The poll loop confirms the diagnosis precisely. Let me check one thing — whether theurge streams child stdout or captures it — since that determines whether the fix is one spot or two.

input.description: Check how theurge engine handles case stdout

content.text:
Architecture's clear now, and it sharpens the concept. The silence has **two layers**:

1. `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` `zrbfc_wait_build_completion` emits per-poll status via `buc_log_args` — **log-only, never console** (`:290`).
2. The theurge engine `rbtdre_run_cases` emits only terminal `PASSED/FAILED` per case (`rbtdre_engine.rs:232`); the case closure itself (`:227 let verdict = (case.func)(&case_dir)`) is an **opaque blocking call** — nothing surfaces while ordain waits on the build.

And the tell: you never saw rbfc's visible `buc_step "Waiting for build completion"` (`:255`) on the console during dogfight, which is strong evidence theurge **captures/redirects the case closure's child stdout** to the trace tree. That's the deliberate quieting from the refactor — clean console, but blind. So the fix can't just be "promote the rbfc log line"; it has to surface *through* theurge.

The right short-mechanical shape: an **engine-side heartbeat** — theurge emits a periodic `running <case> (Ns elapsed)` line while a closure runs, keeping the clean captured-output design while answering "is it progressing?". That's self-contained and rote; the one judgment call is just confirming the stdout-capture assumption and picking the locus.

Let me slate it at the end of ₣BB.

input.content:
# jjezs_slate ordain-poll-progress-heartbeat

## Character
Short, mechanical observability fix. One judgment call (where the emission belongs); the rest is rote.

## Goal
During the silent cloud-build poll window, the operator gets periodic console progress — answering "is it progressing?" — without spelunking rbfc_poll_response_*.json. Wanted live for dogfight, skirmish, gauntlet, any ordain-bearing fixture.

## The silence, two layers
- rbfc_FoundryCore.sh zrbfc_wait_build_completion emits per-poll status via buc_log_args — log-only, never console.
- The theurge engine rbtdre_run_cases emits only terminal PASSED/FAILED per case; the case closure is an opaque blocking call, so nothing surfaces while ordain waits on the build.

## The one judgment call
Confirm theurge captures/redirects the case closure's child stdout (rbfc's visible "Waiting for build completion" buc_step never reached the console during dogfight — strong evidence it does). If captured, the heartbeat belongs engine-side (periodic "running <case> (Ns elapsed)"), not in rbfc — surface progress without un-quieting all child output.

## Wrap criterion
Running dogfight shows periodic build-phase progress on the console during the poll wait.

## Out of scope
Broader theurge trace overhaul; per-step structured progress. Heartbeat only.

Dogfight is **green, first try** — `4 passed, 0 failed`. Both fixtures clean: canonical-invest (`rbtdrk_governor_mantle`, `rbtdrk_retriever_invest`, `rbtdrk_director_invest`) and the dogfight lifecycle case (`rbtdrd_build_run_lifecycle`). And notably it **didn't hit the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID flap this round** — the full chain (mantle → invest → director OAuth → cloud build → poll → vouch → summon → run) is proven healthy on cerebro.

**Yes — kick off skirmish now.** One honest caveat so the expectation is calibrated: skirmish runs its *own* canonical-invest at the start, which re-divests/re-invests the director SA and re-opens the same flap window. So dogfight passing proves the chain works, but it doesn't immunize skirmish — the ~1/3 transient is still live on skirmish's ordain step. If it hits, that's the known flap: re-launch, don't triage.

Launch it in tmux (same shell's free now):

```bash
tmux new -s skirmish
#  inside tmux:
./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.sh          # single attempt, run DIRECTLY — never piped
#  Ctrl-b d to detach, then go offline freely
```

Then you're clear to disconnect. To check on it later from anywhere:

```bash
ssh cerebro 'tmux attach -t skirmish'                                  # live view; Ctrl-b d to leave it running
# or, after it ends, the durable record + exit code:
tail -n 200 ~/projects/logs-buk/same-rbw-ts-skirmish.txt
```

When you're back: reopen a chat and I'll re-orient on ₣BB, read cerebro's skirmish tally (you paste it, or I `jjx_fetch` the log read-only), and if it's green we wrap **₢BBABO** — its docket records that as the green light to retire the four complete rbk- heats. Don't wrap anything until that tally is confirmed green.

Good to launch.

content.text:
Good news on the thing that actually matters: **the run will survive you closing your session.** The ancestry is clean —

```
602697 bud_dispatch (skirmish) → 602683 bash → 22450 tmux: server → 1 systemd
```

It's anchored to a **tmux server** (child of systemd, PID 1), not to your sshd login shell. SIGHUP on disconnect won't reach it. You're safe to close.

One naming wrinkle to note for reattach: there are two tmux clients on that server — one originally launched `-s claude` (PID 22450, now the server) and one `-s skirmish` (602682). I can't tell from process ancestry alone which *named session* holds the run (all session shells are children of the one server). So on reattach, don't assume `-t skirmish` — do **`tmux ls`** first and attach to whichever session is showing the skirmish output. The durable record is the same either way: `~/projects/logs-buk/same-rbw-ts-skirmish.txt`.

**Progress at this peek:**
- ✅ enrollment-validation: **47/47** passed (fixture 1 done)
- ▶ now in canonical-invest (`rbtdrk_governor_mantle`, `rbtdrk_retriever_invest` passed — re-investing the director SA, i.e. re-entering the flap window)
- ⏳ next comes onboarding-sequence — the long, silent cloud-build stretch where the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID transient (if it shows) would land

As asked, this was a one-time peek — **I'm not monitoring** and haven't scheduled anything. Nothing on cerebro depends on this chat.

When you're back, reopen a session and I'll: re-orient on ₣BB, ssh-peek the final skirmish tally, and **if green** → wrap **₢BBABO** (recording it as the green light) and then walk the four rbk- heats for retirement — confirming each paddock is shape-complete before `jjx_archive`. Nothing gets retired until that tally reads clean.

- ✅ The long ~15-min `ordain_conjure_jupyter` build from last peek **completed and passed**
- ▶ Now into the **next ordain build**: latest poll `#9`, status `QUEUED` at 16:07:51 — a fresh build just submitted (bind/graft/airgap-class next)
- `RUN ALIVE`

onboarding-sequence tally so far, all green: `inscribe_reliquary`, `kludge_tadmor`, `kludge_ccyolo`, `ordain_conjure_sentry`, `ordain_conjure_jupyter`. Every credential-flap-exposed step has now ridden through clean. Still ahead after onboarding wraps: regime-validation/smoke, dockerfile-hygiene, then the big crucible tail (tadmor + moriah security cases, srjcl, pluml) — so there's a solid stretch left, but nothing's hit a snag.

Nothing scheduled, not monitoring — the run is server-anchored and independent of this chat.

**When you're back:** reopen a session → I re-orient on ₣BB, ssh-peek the final tally (`tail ~/projects/logs-buk/same-rbw-ts-skirmish.txt` for the `Suite 'skirmish': … passed/failed` line). If green → wrap **₢BBABO** (records the green light) → then walk the four rbk- heats (**₣BT, ₣BQ, ₣BP, ₣BM**), confirm each paddock is shape-complete, and `jjx_archive`. Nothing retired until that tally reads clean.

Safe travels. 👋

content.text:
Confirmed clean. The `RUN ALIVE` was a false positive — `pgrep` was matching *my own ssh command string* (which contains "rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish"); there's no actual skirmish process anymore. The authoritative signals:

- **`BURX_EXIT_STATUS=0`** — clean exit
- **`BURX_ENDED_AT=20260529-164806`** — finished 16:48:06 (started 15:20:14 → **~1h28m wall-clock**)
- **`Suite 'skirmish': 10 fixture(s) run, 234 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped`**

**Skirmish is green, end to end.** 234/234, zero failures, single attempt — the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID flap never bit, all five onboarding ordains rode through, and the crucible tail (tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml) closed clean. This is exactly the wrap criterion ₢BBABO was guarding: *green tally, not "ran to completion."*

So we're at the two downstream actions we lined up — and per wrap discipline I'm not doing either without your word:

1. **Wrap ₢BBABO `skirmish-cerebro-standing-run`?** Summary would record the green skirmish (234/234, exit 0, ~1h28m on cerebro) as the green light to retire the now-complete rbk- heats.
2. **Then vet + retire the four heats** — **₣BT** (rbk-11-mvp-shellcheck-error-gaps), **₣BQ** (rbk-12-harmonize-buc-and-buh), **₣BP** (rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup), **₣BM** (rbk-09-mvp-regime-sprues-misc). I'll read each paddock first to confirm it's shape-complete (not merely pace-complete) before any `jjx_archive`, and surface anything that looks unfinished rather than archiving blind.

Want me to proceed with the wrap of **₢BBABO**, and then move on to vetting the four for retirement?

content.text:
There it is — the run was in the **`skirmish`** session all along (created `15:20:04`, matching your launch). My earlier ancestry read was muddled because the tmux *server* process keeps the cmdline of the first session ever opened (`claude`, from May 23) — but `tmux ls` is authoritative:

```
claude:   1 windows (created Sat May 23 22:11:11 2026)
skirmish: 1 windows (created Fri May 29 15:20:04 2026)   ← your run
```

To reconnect:

```bash
ssh cerebro
tmux attach -t skirmish
#  detach again with:  Ctrl-b  then  d
```

Or as a one-liner from your local terminal (the `-t` forces a pty, which tmux needs):

```bash
ssh -t cerebro tmux attach -t skirmish
```

You'll land on the finished run's scrollback — the `234 passed, 0 failed` summary should be near the bottom. Scroll with `Ctrl-b [` then arrows/PgUp (`q` to exit scroll mode). When you're done with it, you can kill the session with `Ctrl-b` then `:kill-session`, or just detach and leave it.

On macOS Docker Desktop 28.x against tadmor, the sortie returned PASS (SECURE) but mechanism is NOT the iptables layer the docket reasoned about: sentry's PREROUTING NAT chain showed 0 packets matched at dport=8890 after the sortie ran (all three rules — RETURN sentry-IP, RETURN bottle-IP, DNAT — at 0/0). The spoofed SYN never reached sentry. The block is consistent with Docker bridge's per-veth source-IP enforcement at egress (a default Docker security control that drops packets whose source IP does not match the originating container's IP, regardless of CAP_NET_RAW + IP_HDRINCL inside the container's namespace). This is a structural Docker fact in the same family as the anchor memo's 'host attaches to enclave bridge as a peer with bridge gateway IP' finding from the per-IP transition arc — surfaced by empirical testing rather than predicted by architectural reasoning. Consequence captured in the comment: the architectural argument that traded rp_filter=1 for source-IP-flexibility on Docker Desktop delivery is mooted at this specific attack class because Docker's bridge enforcement already blocks below iptables; the iptables layer is not load-bearing for THIS attack class on this runtime, though it remains load-bearing for other attack classes (e.g., the spoof-as-allowed-cidr → forbidden-dst case the existing sortie_net_srcip_spoof covers). The sortie retains regression-backstop value: if a future change (alternative container runtime, custom network mode, raw bridge configuration) removes the Docker bridge enforcement, this sortie would report BREACH and surface the regression. Open: cross-platform verification on Linux Docker Engine and Podman before declaring canonical PASS across the matrix. Notes added in sortie's doc comment (load-bearing — the empirical mechanism differs from docket prediction and a future maintainer reading the docstring needs that context) and a brief pointer comment on the theurge case fn referring back.

  Prove the skirmish suite (mini-gauntlet: standing-depot depot→build→crucible chain, no project churn) runs green on cerebro, as the standing-depot counterpart to the full-gauntlet first-run.
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  Green skirmish tally on cerebro. Not "ran to completion."

  skirmish's canonical-invest + onboarding-sequence ordain path is exposed to the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID re-invest flap (memo-20260527; repair slated separately in this heat). A failure carrying that signature is the known transient — re-run, don't triage. Any other failure is a real surprise: capture and route.

32	    and word-splits it at several call sites. The warning itself questions whether

39	    literal-backslash `case` pattern are likely intentional — candidates for a

# jjezs_paddock ₣BT

## Shape: close the gate, then pay the debt

The defect is a process gap, not a list of warnings. shellcheck qualification
(`buq_shellcheck` — scans every `*.sh` under `Tools/` with `-S style` against
`busc_shellcheckrc`) runs only under release qualify (`rbw-tr`) or the standalone
`buw-qsc` colophon. Nothing on the routine fast path (`rbw-tq`) gates it, so style
debt accumulates invisibly between releases. A baseline sweep surfaced a backlog
that nobody had been shown.

Two halves, in this order: **(1)** fold shellcheck into `rbw-tq` so every fast
qualify gates it and retire the standalone `buw-qsc`; **(2)** drive the surfaced
baseline to zero. Halve-one first is deliberate — once the gate is routine, the
backlog can't silently regrow while we work it.

## The backlog has three textures

Run the shellcheck colophon for the authoritative live list (it drifts as fixes
land — do not trust any enumeration written here). The findings sort into:

- **Mechanical, BCG-canonical** — unquoted numeric `test` operands, unquoted inner
  expansions in `#`/`%` operators, the `("${dir}"/${var}.*)` glob-array quoting
  pattern (same shape as the `buyy_tt_yawp` repair already landed under ₣BQ). Blind-safe.
- **Mechanical and a safety win** — a `rm -rf "/home/${var}"` wanting a `:?` guard.
  Worth doing for its own sake, independent of the warning.
- **Judgment, possibly masking real bugs** — these do NOT get blind-quoted:
  - `rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` carries an array-used-then-assigned-scalar cluster.
    That class can be a genuine data-flow bug, not a style nit — read the whole
    function before touching.
  - `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` stores an `ssh-keygen ... -P '' -f` command in a string
    and word-splits it at several call sites. The warning itself questions whether
    the empty-passphrase `''` survives — a behavioral question to verify, and the
    array fix changes a tinder constant's type across all its sites.
  - The `rbgjb*`/`rbgjr*` Cloud Build job scripts raise an interpreter question
    (pipefail flagged as undefined → read as POSIX `sh`) entangled with intended
    word-splitting. Settle the shebang/interpreter before quoting.
  - A remote-side tilde (`"~/..."` handed to a double-hop ssh pipe) and a
    literal-backslash `case` pattern are likely intentional — candidates for a
    documented `busc_shellcheckrc` entry rather than a code change.

## Locked decisions

- **One routine gate.** shellcheck folds into `rbw-tq`; the standalone `buw-qsc`
  is retired. The BUK `buq_shellcheck` engine stays — only the invocation path
  consolidates. `buq_shellcheck` is BUK-level (reusable), so the engine must not
  gain rbk-specific assumptions.
- **Fixes are opportunistic-correct, not blanket-suppressed.** A finding is silenced
  only when it is a true false-positive, only in `busc_shellcheckrc`, never inline,
  and always with a rationale. Genuine findings get fixed.
- **Judgment findings get investigated.** The `rbfl` and `bujb` clusters are treated
  as bug-or-not questions, resolved explicitly — not quoted away on reflex.

## Leaning, not yet locked

- **Absent-shellcheck behavior on the fast path.** `rbw-tq` is the dependency-light,
  fast qualify; shellcheck is an external tool and scanning the tree costs seconds —
  the likely original reason it lived in release qualify. Lean: **fail when shellcheck
  is present, loud-skip when absent**, so dependency-light machines still pass fast
  qualify while anyone with the tool is gated. Settle this at slate time; it shapes
  whether folding into `rbw-tq` is unconditional.

## Done looks like

- `rbw-tq` runs shellcheck and is green; the standalone `buw-qsc` is gone — a single
  routine gate, no release-only blind spot.
- Every baseline finding is either fixed or a documented `busc_shellcheckrc` survivor
  with rationale.
- The two suspect clusters (`rbfl` array/scalar, `bujb` ssh-keygen empty-passphrase)
  are explicitly resolved as bug or non-bug, with the verdict recorded.

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BQ
2	
3	## Shape: one renderer, two registers
4	
5	The kit has two console-output systems that grew years apart: `buc_*` (Bash
6	Console — operational output during command execution) and `buh_*`/`buym`
7	(handbook prose with the yawp/diastema inline-span concept). They overlap on
8	"color text on a line" but each owns capabilities the other lacks. The genuine
9	duplication is the **color/terminal-capability decision**: `buc` carries its own
10	*unconditional* ANSI palette (latent bug — ignores `NO_COLOR` and `TERM=dumb`),
11	while `buym` already does terminal-aware rendering via `buyf_format_yawp`.
12	
13	The merge: collapse the two palettes/kitchens into one renderer, keep the two
14	public vocabularies. Mental model — **one kitchen, two storefronts.** `buh` is
15	already a thin front-end on the `buym` render core; this heat brings `buc` down
16	to the same structure (a front-end), so both consume one terminal-capability
17	brain.
18	
19	## Locked decisions
20	
21	- **Public signatures unchanged.** No churn across the ~142 `buc_*` files or ~42

24	  `BUYC_*` palette). Its private source-time palette is retired.

29	  with `file:line`); `buh` keeps editorial framing (section headers, body indent,

35	- **`buym` is the renderer's home for now.** Internal naming (the module reads as

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BQ

## Shape: one renderer, two registers

The kit has two console-output systems that grew years apart: `buc_*` (Bash
Console — operational output during command execution) and `buh_*`/`buym`
(handbook prose with the yawp/diastema inline-span concept). They overlap on
"color text on a line" but each owns capabilities the other lacks. The genuine
duplication is the **color/terminal-capability decision**: `buc` carries its own
*unconditional* ANSI palette (latent bug — ignores `NO_COLOR` and `TERM=dumb`),
while `buym` already does terminal-aware rendering via `buyf_format_yawp`.

The merge: collapse the two palettes/kitchens into one renderer, keep the two
public vocabularies. Mental model — **one kitchen, two storefronts.** `buh` is
already a thin front-end on the `buym` render core; this heat brings `buc` down
to the same structure (a front-end), so both consume one terminal-capability
brain.

## Locked decisions

- **Public signatures unchanged.** No churn across the ~142 `buc_*` files or ~42
  `buh_*` files. The merge is internal; call sites are untouched.
- **`buc` becomes a front-end** on the `buym` renderer (`buyf_format_yawp` over the
  `BUYC_*` palette). Its private source-time palette is retired.
- **Deep core.** `buc` messages may carry inline yawp spans (cmd/ui/link), not just
  whole-line color. This is the capability the merge unlocks.
- **Framing stays split — this is the two-register guarantee.** `buc` keeps
  operational framing (gray `ZBUC_CONTEXT` sigil, `BURE_VERBOSE` gating, transcript
  with `file:line`); `buh` keeps editorial framing (section headers, body indent,
  blank-line rhythm). The gray operation prefix lives in `buc` framing and must be
  structurally incapable of reaching handbook prose.
- **Render to two capabilities.** Display path resolves spans to color
  (`buyf_format_yawp`); transcript path resolves them to plain text
  (`buyf_strip_yawp`), so the log carries neither ANSI nor diastema bytes.
- **`buym` is the renderer's home for now.** Internal naming (the module reads as
  "handbook yelp") is a known sin, deferred to post-release.
- **Refactor-first.** `buc` output stays byte-identical except for new
  terminal-awareness. Actually *using* inline spans at `buc` call sites is a
  separate, later appetite — not this heat.

## Locked hazard

`buc` display shifts from source-time color literals to **kindle-time** `BUYC_*`
readonlys, so it now depends on `buym` having kindled. A cold `buc_die` (called
before any kindle) must not throw an unbound-variable error inside the error
path. Guarded via `zbuym_sentinel`; proven by an explicit cold-`buc_die` test.

## Done looks like

- One terminal-capability decision exists in the kit; `buc` and `buh` share it.
- `buc` output respects `NO_COLOR` and `TERM=dumb` (the latent bug is fixed).
- The duplicated tabtarget-glob (`buc_tabtarget` vs `buyy_tt_yawp`) is collapsed to
  one resolver.
- Full `fast` suite green; sampled operational *and* handbook output verified under
  `NO_COLOR=1` and `TERM=dumb`.

content.text: ₣BQ shape-complete: "Done looks like" = unified terminal-capability decision shared by `buc`/`buh`, `NO_COLOR`/`TERM=dumb` respected, duplicated tabtarget-glob collapsed, fast suite green. 5/5 closed. The one deferral (buym internal naming "known sin → post-release") is explicit and non-blocking. Clear. Next — ₣BP:

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BP
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.
6	
7	The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.
8	
9	## Locked decisions
10	
11	- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
12	- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
13	- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All four co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
14	- **Co-maintained constant set in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earn a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.
15	- **Fixture names are Rust-authored — out of the projection.** On inspection, fixtures are defined in Rust (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry; fixture structs name themselves), and bash holds only redundant copies in its `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` suite arrays — there is no canonical bash author, so fixtures fail the single-author rule. The redundancy is removed in the other direction: suite definitions relocate into theurge (Rust) and bash becomes a pure forwarder. This is an ownership relocation, not a bash→Rust constant projection — a future pace must not re-attempt projecting fixture names.
16	
17	## What done looks like
18	
19	The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).
20

# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All four co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Co-maintained constant set in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earn a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.
- **Fixture names are Rust-authored — out of the projection.** On inspection, fixtures are defined in Rust (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry; fixture structs name themselves), and bash holds only redundant copies in its `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` suite arrays — there is no canonical bash author, so fixtures fail the single-author rule. The redundancy is removed in the other direction: suite definitions relocate into theurge (Rust) and bash becomes a pure forwarder. This is an ownership relocation, not a bash→Rust constant projection — a future pace must not re-attempt projecting fixture names.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).
